items.rationale: His dominant 2024 focus; sees a fast, simple, extensible zkVM as a public good that scales blockchains and brings more computation onchain. Active roadmap (Binius integration) to make proving much faster.

citations.quote: Today we're sharing our biggest open source release yet: Jolt Alpha, our zkVM built new from the ground up.
Binius pairs exceptionally well with Jolt, and it's a top priority on our roadmap. The simplest, most performant zkVM will get much, much faster.

items.rationale: Believes private, decentralized proof-of-personhood is a critical prerequisite for crypto adoption and for defending networks against AI bots; sybil filtering keeps airdrops valuable and stakeholders aligned.

citations.quote: How bad does this need to get before all networks need to integrate a private proof of personhood system like @worldnetwork?
Filtering Sybils keeps airdrops valuable and decentralized.

items.rationale: Argues the coming flood of AI-generated content can only be addressed with cryptography and blockchains via provenance/attestation graphs plus sybil resistance; sees no serious non-crypto solution.

citations.quote: Every robust method to prove the origin or history of content involves cryptography and blockchains.
Crypto skeptics should explain how they think we're going to solve this problem. I don't think it can be done seriously without crypto.

items.rationale: Long-running thesis that open, permissionless networks resist centralization and give users true ownership of usernames, follower graphs and data, unlike corporate web2 networks.

citations.quote: The amazing team at Late Checkout released a dapp on the new River protocol (user-owned chat communities)... I dig it.
You don't own your username — or anything else — on a corporate network.

items.rationale: Sees payments as an original crypto goal now becoming viable; cheap mobile USDC transfers could save merchants ~30% vs app-store fees and create direct user relationships.

citations.quote: Payments has been one of the goals of crypto from the beginning. The pieces are finally coming together.
If an app can collect payment from a mobile crypto wallet... merchants could save ~30% and maintain a more direct relationship with users.

items.rationale: Champions light-client tech to reduce trust assumptions and client diversity for L2 robustness; wants Helios shipped by default in wallets and extended to L2s. Scaling is a named prerequisite for adoption.

citations.quote: These are the top priorities for Helios. If you need support... or would like to contribute, please let me and @NoahCitron know!
I personally think scaling, privacy, and something relating to key management/auth/identity are critical prerequisites.

items.rationale: Repeatedly highlights app/account-layer privacy work (Cicada, Nocturne, zkdrops) and insists mainstream crypto and ID need privacy, paired with compliance-provability.

citations.quote: (And privacy is coming to crypto.)
should there be a programmable, private ID under decentralized control

items.rationale: Excited by an onchain remix graph enabling recursive, networked creativity where original creators are compensated for downstream reuse — 'multiplayer for IP'.
items.age_note: Stated Sept 2023, not restated through mid-2024; conviction inferred to persist.

citations.quote: Story Protocol fixes this with an onchain remix graph that makes composition and attribution natural.

items.rationale: Wants symbolic testing and formal methods to make Ethereum safer; backs Halmos and the Security Alliance / Whitehat Safe Harbor to build new security tools for crypto.

citations.quote: Crypto is unique, and fulfilling its promise requires developing new legal and technical tools for security researchers.
We hope Halmos will be the best symbolic testing tool in Ethereum.

items.rationale: Enthusiastic about research-lab designs enabling trustless data markets and efficient proofs (fair data exchange, naysayer proofs, cleaner fixed/variable-rate AMMs).

citations.quote: With FDE, you could set up a trustless market to buy data.
The DELV team went deep and emerged with the cleanest fixed/variable rate AMM design yet.

items.rationale: Sees account abstraction unlocking 'smart accounts' that combine identity and ownership and enable counter-intuitive new capabilities beyond simple wallets.
items.age_note: Active interest mid-2023; not prominent in 2024 statements.

citations.quote: Account abstraction enables smart accounts. What are some counter-intuitive possibilities enabled by smart accounts?

items.rationale: His sharpest recent concern: memecoins make crypto look like a risky casino, deter great founders, give politicized regulators ammunition, and aren't technically interesting — actively setting the industry back.

citations.quote: we shouldn't pretend that the casino doesn't set us back. Deterring great founders and giving ammo to politicized regulators has a profound effect.
Memecoins alter how the public, regulators, and entrepreneurs see crypto. At best, it looks like a risky casino.

items.rationale: Worries that closed corporate networks block permissionless innovation and that naive p2p designs degrade back to centralization; only architectures that resist centralization preserve user freedom.

citations.quote: Because of its network architecture, Nostr likely degrades to centralization — like Fediverse. This happens often in simple p2p systems.
LinkedIn (like many others) is a closed, permissioned network where no real experimentation is possible.

items.rationale: Expects most online content to soon be machine-generated, with everything assumed fake unless accompanied by provenance/proofs — a societal trust problem he views as nearly unavoidable.

citations.quote: we aren't far from the internet devolving into a place where *most* content has been generated (or can be generated on demand).
The "mystery meat" scenario seems unavoidable

items.rationale: Critical of draconian, expensive app-store policies and Apple's fee structures; sees them as a tax and a constraint that crypto-native payments and browsers could route around.

citations.quote: Apple's EU pricing is actually better for apps with fewer, higher-paying users and unworkable for apps with many free users.
"App Store policies are draconian and expensive."

items.rationale: Frustrated that centralized control of AI (e.g. gated model weights, org-culture-shaped model behavior) slows innovation; favors open-source/uncensored models and decentralized alternatives.

citations.quote: No e/acc nocoiner I know... adequately appreciates the ways centralization limits the speed of innovation.
Sharing only with governments and academics (requiring a form to be filled out!) was nonsense.

items.rationale: Thinks Bitcoin's security model must change to survive coming decades, and that it needs upgrades (e.g. a VERIFY_SNARK opcode) to become programmable enough to realize Satoshi's vision.

citations.quote: the security model of bitcoin needs to change if it's going to last the next few decades.
adding a VERIFY_SNARK opcode to the bitcoin protocol — would make bitcoin totally programmable

items.rationale: Believes most promising-but-infeasible crypto ideas (UX, onchain games, graph/social data, complex markets) are bottlenecked primarily by compute — implicitly motivating his ZK/scaling work.

citations.quote: 90% of the unfeasible but good ideas I hear are primarily limited by compute.

items.rationale: Repeatedly endorses Worldcoin's hardware-biometric sybil resistance as a necessary complement to trust/social graphs and a prerequisite for networks to survive AI bots. Note: excitement is about the personhood system, not token-price speculation.

citations.quote: Trust graph is much more robust coupled with a sybil resistance mechanism, which is what Worldcoin provides.
all networks need to integrate a private proof of personhood system like @worldnetwork?

items.rationale: Views memecoins broadly as net-negative: technically uninteresting, extracting rather than creating value, and harming crypto's reputation, regulation, and founder quality. No single token named — concern is the whole category.
items.age_note: Interest expected to 'come and go'; he treats the category as cyclical noise rather than a durable trend.

citations.quote: the meme is actually extracting (and not creating) value. strong conceptual fit. wow
memecoins aren't very technically interesting. It shouldn't be a surprise that they're not attractive to builders.

risk_regime.rationale: Structurally and persistently bullish on crypto's underlying technology and on building through cycles — 'never been a better time to build', payments 'pieces finally coming together' — while explicitly rejecting speculative/casino risk-taking. His risk appetite is for long-horizon technical/infrastructure bets, not market timing or trading; he expresses no tactical price view.

notes: Eddy is an engineering/research-driven builder (a16z crypto CTO), not a token picker — his 'market view' is overwhelmingly about protocols, infrastructure, and tech primitives rather than tradable assets, so tokens lists are deliberately sparse. Most recent (2024) signal is heavily weighted toward zkVMs/Jolt (his flagship work) and an unusually pointed, sustained anti-memecoin stance in April 2024. No major reversals detected; older stances (Story Protocol IP, account abstraction, Bitcoin security) are marked persisted where not restated. He consistently couples enthusiasm with a builder-vs-speculator framing: technology = exciting, casino = concerning. Terminology note: he deliberately abandoned 'Data Availability' for 'Data Publishing/Storage' (2023-09-15), a small sign he tracks precision closely. Risk regime is structural optimism, not a tactical market call — he offers no view on near-term prices.
